lesson.menu_desc: Chair and steer a meeting from start to finish: open with a clear purpose and agenda, manage the floor by bringing quiet people in and cutting dominators off politely, park digressions and sidebars, keep to time, drive the room to a decision, and close with a recap and owners — using chairing language: soft imperatives (shall we / why don't we + bare verb), polite control (would you mind + -ing, could we + verb), and steering markers (let's move on, can we come back to that, in the interest of time) — 33 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 17 — Leading the Room — Chairing & Steering a Meeting
lesson.title_tag: Professor View — Victor Malvezi Paschotto | Aula 17 — Leading the Room

listenings.text: Right, let me think through how I chair this one before I walk in, because last quarter the cross-regional call ran forty minutes over and we left with no decision. Not again. First, I open with the purpose, not small talk. One line: we are here to lock the rollout date, and we leave with an owner for each region. That frames the whole hour. Second, I run the agenda visibly. Three items, a time box on each, and I say it out loud so the room polices itself. If we have quorum, we decide; if we do not, we note it and move on. Third, I manage the floor like a job, because it is one. The two loud ones will eat the hour if I let them, so when one digresses I park it: that is a good point, let us put it in a sidebar and come back to it. And the quiet regional leads always have the real information, so I bring them in by name: Carla, you run that corridor, what is your read? Fourth, I protect the time. The moment we drift, I say, in the interest of time, can we come back to that after, and I steer us back to the decision. And I never let it end loose. I close with a recap: here is what we decided, here is who owns what, here is the date. Then I adjourn on time. Purpose up front, agenda with time boxes, bring the quiet in and park the loud, guard the clock, recap and adjourn. That is chairing, not just attending.

Hi Victor, it's Beatriz. Quick voice note before you chair the cross-regional review, because running a room is a completely different muscle from being good in one, and most strong managers never learn it. Three things. First, the chair owns the purpose and the clock, nobody else. Open with why we are here and what a finished meeting looks like, put a time box on every item, and say it out loud so the group keeps itself honest. A meeting with no stated purpose drifts every single time. Second, your real job is the floor, not the content. The confident voices will dominate and the people with the actual answers will stay silent, so steer deliberately: park a digression instead of fighting it, push a side conversation into a sidebar for later, and bring the quiet ones in by name with a direct, easy question. You are not being rude, you are doing the job. Third, never let a meeting dissolve. Before anyone leaves, recap the decisions, name an owner for each action, confirm the date, and adjourn cleanly. If people walk out unsure what was decided, the meeting did not happen. Own the purpose, guard the clock, run the floor, and close with a recap and owners. Do that and people will actually want you in the chair.
